Tafseer:
1- Historical Status of this Verse
A study of the Holy Quran reveals that the theme of this verse has been passed down among people of religion since ancient times. These eloquent words may have been revealed for the first time in the Quran, but as far as its meaning is concerned, it is so perfectly suited for the commencement of any task that the heart testifies that Allah must have taught it to mankind right from the very beginning. Thus, the Quran itself records regarding Prophet Noah (peace be upon him) that when he boarded his believing family and companions onto the ark, he uttered similar words:
'And he said, "Embark therein; in the name of Allah is its course and its anchorage. Indeed, my Lord is Forgiving and Merciful."' (Hud - 41)
Similarly, Prophet Solomon (peace be upon him) began the letter he wrote to the Queen of Sheba with these same blessed words. The Quran states:
'Indeed, it is from Solomon, and indeed, it reads: In the name of Allah, the Entirely Merciful, the Especially Merciful.' (An-Naml - 30)

2- This Verse is a Supplication (Dua)
This is not merely an informative statement, but like Surah Al-Fatihah, as will be explained later, it is a supplication. It is the natural call of a person with a sound nature, which should be uttered before starting any significant task. Divine revelation has clothed this natural call in words, and such beautiful words that a better expression cannot be imagined. When this supplication is uttered with intention and consciousness before starting a task, firstly, it warns a person at the very first step that the task they are about to undertake must not be in disobedience or rebellion against God, but should be in accordance with His pleasure and under His commands. Secondly, through the blessing of this supplication, one gains the support of God's two great attributes, Rahman (Entirely Merciful) and Rahim (Especially Merciful). These two aspects guarantee that Allah will bless the task, protect them from the evil consequences of any mistakes made in taking it up, give them the strength and courage to sustain and complete it, keep them safe from the plots and deceptions of Satan, and make the task beneficial and blessed for them in this world, and a means of attaining God's pleasure in the Hereafter. Any task done without this supplication is devoid of all these blessings, which is why the Holy Prophet (PBUH) said that any task not started with 'Bismillah-ir-Rahman-ir-Rahim' is devoid of blessings.
While these blessings apply to every task, starting the recitation of the Quran with this supplication has additional aspects to consider.
First, by starting the recitation of the Quran with Bismillah, the servant complies with the command Allah gave the Prophet (PBUH) at the time of the very first revelation: 'Read in the name of your Lord who created.' (Al-Alaq - 1).
Second, this blessed phrase reminds us that Allah's greatest favor upon humanity is granting them the ability to speak and articulate, making them worthy of the blessing of the Quran. The attribute of Rahman points towards this reality. Elsewhere, it is explicitly stated that it is Allah's mercy that He created man, gave him speech, and taught him the Quran: 'The Most Merciful. Taught the Quran. Created man. Taught him eloquence.' (Ar-Rahman 1-4).
Third, this verse confirms a specific prophecy regarding the Prophet (PBUH) and the Quran, found in previous scriptures. It states that the teachings he gives to God's creation will be in the name of God. In the fifth book of Moses (Deuteronomy 18:18-19), these words are recorded: 'I will raise up for them a prophet like you from among their brothers; I will put my words in his mouth, and he will tell them everything I command him. If anyone does not listen to my words that the prophet speaks in my name, I myself will call him to account.'
Fourth, just as the Quran is a manifestation of God's attribute of Rahmaniyyat (mercy), it is His mercy that is the key to unlocking the Quran. It will open its closed doors, ease its difficulties, shower the reader with meanings and truths from this source of grace, and protect them from deviation and the evils of the self and Satan.

3- The Beautiful Names in the Verse
Three of Allah's names are mentioned in this verse: Allah, Rahman, Rahim. Let us briefly understand their meanings.
Allah:
The name Allah is formed by prefixing the definite article 'Al' to the word 'Ilah'. From the beginning, this name has been exclusively reserved for the Supreme God, the Creator of the heavens, the earth, and all creatures. Even before the revelation of the Quran, this was its meaning in pre-Islamic Arabia. Despite being polytheists, the Arabs did not consider any of their deities equal to God. They acknowledged that Allah alone is the Creator of the heavens and the earth, the maker of the sun and moon, the one who subjects them, and the one who sends down rain and provides sustenance. They worshipped other deities merely under the false assumption that these were close to Allah and would intercede for them. Their beliefs are detailed in the Quran. For brevity, we quote a couple of verses:
'We only worship them that they may bring us nearer to Allah in position.' (Az-Zumar - 3)
'And if you asked them, "Who created the heavens and earth and subjected the sun and the moon?" they would surely say, "Allah." Then how are they deluded? Allah extends provision for whom He wills of His servants and restricts for him. Indeed Allah is, of all things, Knowing. And if you asked them, "Who sends down rain from the sky and gives life thereby to the earth after its lifelessness?" they would surely say, "Allah."' (Al-Ankabut 61-63).
Similarly, they recognized Allah as the true source and center of all powers, life and death, and the administration of the universe.

Rahman and Rahim:
The name Rahman is an intensive form (Mubalaghah) on the pattern of Ghadban and Sakran. And Rahim is an adjective on the pattern of Alim and Karim. Some people think Rahman has more intensity than Rahim, and thus consider Rahim a redundant word added merely for emphasis. In our view, this is incorrect. In Arabic usage, the pattern of Fa'lan denotes passion, enthusiasm, and surging emotion, while the pattern of Fa'il denotes continuity, permanence, and stability. Therefore, neither is superfluous; one expresses the surging, overflowing nature of God's mercy, and the other its continuity and permanence. Reflect upon it, and you will see that God's mercy on His creation is exactly of this nature. He did not just passionately create the world and then neglect it; rather, after creating it, He nurtures and cares for it with His complete attribute of Rahimiyyat. Whenever a servant calls upon Him, He hears and answers their prayers. His mercies are not limited to this brief worldly life, but those who follow His path will enjoy His mercy in eternal, unending life. This entire reality cannot be expressed unless both words are used together.

4- The Position of this Verse in the Quran
An important question arises: what is the actual place of this verse in the Quran? It is written as an independent verse at the beginning of every Surah (except Surah At-Tawbah), but it doesn't seem to be a part of any Surah (except Surah An-Naml). Because of this, there is a difference of opinion on whether it is a part of any specific Surah or merely a blessed beginning and a separator. The reciters and jurists of Medina, Basra, and Syria opine that it is not a verse of any Surah, including Al-Fatihah, but is written at the start merely for blessings and as a separator. This is also the view of Imam Abu Hanifa (RA).
Conversely, the jurists of Mecca and Kufa consider it a verse of Surah Al-Fatihah and other Surahs. This is the view of Imam Shafi'i (RA) and his companions.
Ustadh Imam Maulana Hamiduddin Farahi (RA) considered it a verse of Surah Al-Fatihah and acting as an opening for other Surahs. The view of the reciters of Medina seems stronger to me, as the current compilation of the Mushaf was done entirely under divine guidance and the Prophet's instructions, and the writing of Bismillah is part of that arrangement, where it is treated equally as an independent verse acting as a separator.

Hamd: The word 'Hamd' is generally translated by Quran translators as 'praise'. However, I have translated it as 'gratitude/thanks' (Shukr). The reason is that wherever this word is used in this combination in the Quran, it conveys the meaning of gratitude. For example, 'And they will say, "Praise/Thanks be to Allah, who has guided us to this"' (Al-A'raf - 43). 'And the last of their call will be, "Praise/Thanks to Allah, Lord of the worlds!"' (Yunus - 10). 'Praise/Thanks to Allah, who has granted to me in old age Ishmael and Isaac.' (Ibrahim - 39).
Although 'Hamd' is broader than 'Shukr' (Shukr is used only when acknowledging qualities or favors that directly benefit the person, whereas Hamd is general for all good qualities whether they benefit the person or not), the essence of gratitude is dominant in this word. To do justice to its translation, one must either combine 'praise' with 'thanks', or use 'thanks' to better reflect the feeling of gratitude and thankfulness that this Surah embodies. Merely saying 'praise' doesn't fully capture this. A person can praise any good thing even if it has nothing to do with them, but the passion in this Surah arises from witnessing Allah's Lordship and Mercy that directly relate to us. Translating it as 'thanks' highlights this aspect of the Surah.
Allah: This has already been explained under the verse of Bismillah.
Rabb: 'Rabb' means the Sustainer/Nourisher and the Master/Lord. The second meaning naturally follows from the first, because the one who sustains has the right to be the Master. This meaning has become so dominant that it is rarely used solely for 'nurturing' in isolation.
The primary audience of the Quran, as mentioned earlier, believed Allah alone to be the Creator of the universe. However, they had set up other 'lords' whom they thought God had made partners in running the universe, and thus deemed them worthy of worship and obedience. Here, right after Allah, His first attribute mentioned is 'Lord of the Worlds' to clarify that the Creator is also the sole Master, because He alone sustains everyone.

The name Rahman is an intensive form (Mubalaghah) on the pattern of Ghadban and Sakran. And Rahim is an adjective on the pattern of Alim and Karim. Some people think Rahman has more intensity than Rahim, and thus consider Rahim a redundant word added merely for emphasis. In our view, this is incorrect. In Arabic usage, the pattern of Fa'lan denotes passion, enthusiasm, and surging emotion, while the pattern of Fa'il denotes continuity, permanence, and stability. Therefore, neither is superfluous; one expresses the surging, overflowing nature of God's mercy, and the other its continuity and permanence. Reflect upon it, and you will see that God's mercy on His creation is exactly of this nature. He did not just passionately create the world and then neglect it; rather, after creating it, He nurtures and cares for it with His complete attribute of Rahimiyyat. Whenever a servant calls upon Him, He hears and answers their prayers. His mercies are not limited to this brief worldly life, but those who follow His path will enjoy His mercy in eternal, unending life. This entire reality cannot be expressed unless both words are used together.

Meanings of the word 'Deen': The word 'Deen' is used in the Quran in several meanings.
1. Religion and Law: e.g., 'Do they desire other than the religion of Allah?' (Al-Imran - 83).
2. Law of the Land: e.g., 'He could not take his brother within the religion (law) of the king.' (Yusuf - 76).
3. Obedience: e.g., 'To Him belongs whatever is in the heavens and the earth, and to Him is obedience due always.' (An-Nahl - 52).
4. Recompense (Reward/Punishment): e.g., 'Indeed, what you are promised is true. And indeed, the recompense is to occur.' (Ad-Dhariyat 5-6).
Recompense includes both aspects: the reward for good deeds and the punishment for evil ones. Therefore, in the translation, we have included the words 'reward and punishment'.
Being the sole Master of the Day of Recompense means that on that day, all power and authority will belong exclusively to Him. Everyone will be helpless and bow down before Him. No one will dare to speak without His permission. He alone will decide all matters, punishing whom He wills and rewarding whom He wills. As stated:
'Sovereignty, that Day, is for Allah; He will judge between them.' (Al-Hajj - 56).
'To whom belongs [all] sovereignty this Day? To Allah, the One, the Prevailing.' (Ghafir - 16).
The profound meaning hidden in the three words of this verse, if expanded, means: A day of reward and punishment is coming. On that day, all authority will rest solely with Allah, and no one will be able to utter a word. However, in the supplicatory style of the verse, this is wrapped in such a way that the supplicant acknowledges all these truths as an established fact. It implies that after the signs and proofs of God's Lordship, Mercy, and Justice spread throughout the universe, who but a stubborn denier could reject any part of this reality?

Concept of 'Ibadah' (Worship): In Arabic lexicon, 'Ibadah' originally means expressing the utmost humility and submissiveness. However, in the Quran, this word has become specific to the humility and reverence a servant shows towards their Creator and Master. Furthermore, the concept of obedience has become an integral part of this word, because it is self-evidently absurd for a person to consider a Being worthy of their utmost devotion yet not deem obedience to Him necessary in the affairs of life. The Quran clarifies this essence of worship in several places. For example:
'Indeed, We have sent down to you the Book, [O Muhammad], in truth. So worship Allah, [being] exclusive to Him in religion.' (Az-Zumar - 2).
This connection between worship and obedience is so deep that in some places, the word is used purely to mean obedience. For example:
'Did I not enjoin upon you, O children of Adam, that you not worship Satan - [for] indeed, he is to you a clear enemy.' (Ya-Sin - 60).
The right of Allah over His servants is stated in this verse, as well as the right the servant has been granted by Allah. Allah's right is that the servant should worship Him alone and seek help from Him alone. The right Allah has taken upon Himself for the servant is that He descends His mercy upon them and helps them. In the first part of the verse, the servant acknowledges Allah's right, and in the second part, they present a request for the right Allah has promised. But the manner of presenting it is highly respectful. Instead of claiming any right, the servant merely expresses their need, their trust, and their desire. It is fitting for a servant to beseech and request, not to assert a right over their Lord. It is entirely Allah's grace and bounty that He grants everything to the servant without them having any inherent entitlement, and then calls this grace the servant's 'right'. Regarding this Surah, in a famous Hadith Qudsi, the Holy Prophet (PBUH) quoted Allah's words specifically about this segment: When the servant says 'It is You we worship and You we ask for help', Allah says, 'This is divided between Me and My servant, and My servant shall have what he asked for.'
The words 'we ask for help' are general. Therefore, this seeking of help can be for worship specifically, and for other worldly affairs. In worship, a servant needs God's help for guidance, strength, and steadfastness, because worship—especially when it encompasses obedience to God in every aspect of life—is a great trial. It includes tough situations where even the greatest waver.
Placing the object first ('Iyyaka' - You alone) creates exclusivity. That is, worship is exclusively for God, and help is sought exclusively from Him. This exclusivity entirely cuts off all ties of polytheism (shirk). After this confession, the servant has nothing to give to anyone other than Allah, nor any room to ask from anyone else. The only permissible relationships with others are those established by Allah Himself.

'Ihdina' (Guide us) does not simply mean 'show us the straight path', but its meaning is much broader. It also means: make our hearts content with the correctness of this path, instill in us the passion and desire to walk upon it, make its difficulties easy for us, and after placing us on it, protect us from wandering off onto other trails. This entire meaning is derived here by the omission of a preposition.
In 'As-Sirat Al-Mustaqim', the 'Al' (definite article) indicates a specific known path. It refers to the straight path that Allah Himself has opened for His servants, which guarantees success in both religion and the world, the path that Prophets and Messengers invited people to, the path upon which God's righteous servants have always walked, which is the closest and easiest. Though misguided people and misguiders have later invented many crooked and winding paths, the straight path remains intact, and those who reach God can only do so by walking upon it. The Prophet (PBUH) once explained this straight path by drawing a straight line on the ground, then drawing intersecting and crooked lines to its right and left, and saying: 'This (straight line) is the path of Allah, and these intersecting lines are trails, and at the head of every one of these trails is a devil calling towards it.'

'The path of those upon whom You have bestowed favor...': The more deeply a person is attached to something, the more clearly they want to understand it themselves and explain it to others. Therefore, the supplicant does not just stop at 'Guide us to the straight path', but fully clarifies it, both positively and negatively. The positive aspect is that it should be the path of those who received Your favor, and the negative aspect is that they should not be those who earned Your anger, nor those who went astray. After this clarification, the objective becomes so crystal clear that no room for ambiguity remains.
This entire clarification was not needed because (God forbid) there was any chance of Allah misunderstanding the prayer's intent. Rather, the seeker is expressing their disassociation from those who turned away from or lost the True Beloved, alongside seeking their true goal. The seeker is also asking for steadfastness and stability so that after finding this path, they remain on it, and do not end up like those who, after receiving the path, willfully deviated from it and earned God's wrath, or those who, due to their fondness for innovation, found the path but then lost it.
This verse mentions three groups: 1. The Favored (Mun'am Alaihim), 2. Those who earned anger (Maghdhoob Alaihim), 3. Those who went astray (Dhaalleen). Let us briefly understand the characteristics of these three groups.
'An'amta Alaihim' (Upon whom You bestowed favor): 'Favor' here primarily refers to the blessing of guidance and the Divine Law (Shariah), through which a person discovers the path to success in both this world and the Hereafter. The act of 'bestowing favor' is used here in its complete and truest sense. It refers to those to whom Allah granted the blessing of Shariah, and they accepted it wholeheartedly. They remained grateful to Allah for this blessing, valued it themselves, and encouraged others to value it. They dedicated their energies and abilities to protect it, sacrificed wealth, and if necessary, did not hesitate to sacrifice their lives in its path. Here, the statement is concise, so it isn't explicitly clear who this group refers to, but another verse clarifies this favored group:
'And whoever obeys Allah and the Messenger - those will be with the ones upon whom Allah has bestowed favor of the prophets, the steadfast affirmers of truth, the martyrs and the righteous.' (An-Nisa - 69).
'Maghdhoobi Alaihim' (Those who earned anger): Here, the action (anger) is not directly attributed to Allah in the grammatical structure, unlike the mention of 'favor'. One reason for this is out of respect (to not directly attribute anger to God in the same breath as favor), and the second is that 'favor' is always and in every circumstance upon the servant from Allah alone, whereas a servant makes themselves deserving of God's wrath through their own actions.
Who are the 'Maghdhoob Alaihim'?: This refers to two types of people. First, those upon whom Allah sent down the blessing of His Shariah, but due to their arrogance and rebellion, they not only rejected it but stood up in opposition to it, and pursued and killed those who presented it to them. As a consequence, God's wrath descended upon them and they were destroyed.
Second, those who accepted it, but not with a willing heart; rather, they accepted it reluctantly. Then, quickly falling into carnal desires, they wasted parts of it, tampered with other parts to suit their desires, and when people tried to correct them or bring them back to the right path, they belied some and killed others. Among past nations, the clearest example of this is the Jews. The Quran explicitly mentions them incurring wrath. For example:
'Those whom Allah has cursed and with whom He became angry and made of them apes and pigs...' (Al-Ma'idah - 60).
'And they were covered with humiliation and poverty and returned with anger from Allah.' (Al-Baqarah - 61).
The Reality of 'Dhaalleen' (Those who went astray): This refers to those who went to extremes in their religion, who elevated the status of their prophet so much that they made him God, who were not content with only the acts of worship and obedience prescribed by Allah and His Messenger, but invented a whole system of monasticism from their own minds, who blindly followed the innovations and errors of their predecessors, and thus deviated from the straight path onto the trails of misguidance. Among past nations, the clearest example of this is the Christians. For these very reasons, the Quran has declared them as misguided and those who misguide others. For example:
'Say, "O People of the Scripture, do not exceed limits in your religion beyond the truth and do not follow the inclinations of a people who had gone astray before and misled many and have strayed from the soundness of the way."' (Al-Ma'idah - 77).
